macro_segments.title: Me'un Following Get: Whether a Second Husband's Me'un Redefines the Woman's Status Vis-à-vis the First Husband

micro_segments.title: Setting up the Mishnah's Apparent Contradiction: First Case Suggests Me'un of Second Husband Redefines Status
The Mishnah's Conclusion: Me'un of Second Husband Does NOT Redefine Status Vis-à-vis First Husband
Ulla's Resolution: The Case of Three Gittin Makes Clear That Ge'rushat Status Becomes Fixed After Multiple Divorces
The Historical Testimony: Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Yehuda ben Beteira Forbid Return; Rabbi Yishmael Permits
Rabbi Yishmael bar Rabbi Yose's Position: Me'un of Second Husband Does Redefine Status and Permits Return to First Husband

macro_segments.title: Divorce and Remarriage: Whether Second Marriage Redefines a Woman's Status for Yibum Purposes

micro_segments.title: Determining When Me'un is Permitted After Death: The Question of Retroactive Me'un in Fulfillment of Mitzvah

macro_segments.title: Me'un Following Get: Whether a Second Husband's Me'un Redefines the Woman's Status Vis-à-vis the First Husband (continued)

micro_segments.title: Resolving the Dispute Through Practical Consequences: She May Marry the First Husband's Brother but Not Return to Him

macro_segments.title: Divorce and Remarriage: Whether Second Marriage Redefines a Woman's Status for Yibum Purposes (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Simple Case: Two Adult Sisters Married to Two Brothers; One Husband Dies
The Disputed Cases: Adult Woman Divorced and Remarried; Does Second Marriage Redefine Her Status?
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Divorce Creates Permanent Ge'rushat Achiv Status Even After Remarriage
The Gemara's Explanation of Rabbi Eliezer: A Gezeirah to Protect Against the Serious Case of Father-Married Then Self-Remarried Minor
The Critical Third Case: Minor Married by Father, Then Divorced, Then Remarried as Minor—Everyone Agrees She Remains Ge'rushat Achiv

macro_segments.title: Me'un and Yibum in Conflict: Two Sisters Married to Two Brothers, One Husband Dies

micro_segments.title: The Setup: Two Minor Sisters Married to Two Brothers; One Brother Dies
Simple Scenarios: When One Sister Is Adult or Deaf, the Other Is Minor or Deaf
The Dilemma: One Sister Adult, One Minor; The Adult's Husband Dies—Conflicting De'oraita and D'rabbanan Obligations
Rabbi Eliezer's Solution: Teach the Minor to Perform Me'un, Freeing the Surviving Brother for De'oraita Yibum
Rabban Gamliel's Solution: Allow Voluntary Me'un or Wait Until Minor Becomes Adult; No Concern for Achot Zekukato
Rabbi Yehoshua's Position: Impossible Situation—Must Divorce One Wife and Perform Chalitzah with the Other
The Gemara's Concern: Is It Permissible to Teach a Woman to Perform Me'un? Exception When It Enables De'oraita Yibum

macro_segments.title: Rabbinic Values: Preferring Chalitzah, Pursuing Peace, Annulling Vows, and the Importance of Translating Learning into Action

micro_segments.title: Bar Kapparah's Teaching: Three Things to Cleave To and Three to Distance From—Including Preference for Chalitzah Over Yibum
Abba Shaul on Yibum: Only Valid When Motivated by Honoring Brother's Name; Otherwise the Child is Quasi-Mamzer
The Imperative of Peace: Seeking and Pursuing Shalom as a Fundamental Religious Value
Annulling Vows: Personal Religiosity vs. Communal Institutions—The Problem of Vows and Personal Altars
Cosigners and the Hidden Judge: Why One Should Distance from Collateral, Converts, and Being a Judge Who Oversteps Expertise
The Danger of Gerim: Negative Statements About Accepting Converts, Mitigated by Tosafot's Nuanced Contextualization
Learning Must Translate to Action: The Principle That Torah Without Doing Has No Reward
The Judge's Responsibility: The Weight of Judgment and the Danger of Narrow Interpretation Without Seeking Guidance
